text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
Northborough Community Hospital  
Neurosciences Department, 12 Riverside Way, Northborough, NB4 2JT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Mr Harold Bennett, DOB: 14-11-1941, Hospital No: H3928471 NHS No. 7845209631  
Flat 2, Willow Court, 7 Market Lane, Northborough, NB3 1QD  

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder of uncertain classification. 

I reviewed Mr Harold Bennett in the neurology clinic today via a joint bedside assessment at home, with his primary nurse-carer in attendance and contributing most of the interval history as he is bedbound. The care team report that daily observations and a seizure log have been maintained. On Carbamazepine monotherapy he was seizure-free for 4 months, until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago. This episode lasted approximately 90 seconds, with behavioural arrest, right hand automatisms, and post-event confusion for around 15 minutes. There was no witnessed generalisation, no tongue biting, and no incontinence. No rescue medication was required. There have been no further definite events since that date.

Background history indicates reduced mobility following a prolonged admission with pneumonia earlier this year; he has remained bedbound since discharge and requires full assistance with personal care. Nutrition and hydration are supervised; there have been no missed doses reported. The nurse notes that sleep is fragmented due to nocturia, and that there has been a recent change to a new care rota which may have increased evening fatigue. No recent head injuries, intercurrent infections, or new medications were reported. There is no alcohol use. 

Current anti-seizure therapy: Carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily (monotherapy). The nurse confirms excellent adherence using a dosette system with signed administration records. No clear adverse effects have been observed apart from mild daytime drowsiness that does not appear to fluctuate with dosing times. 

Examination today was limited by his bedbound status. He was alert and oriented to person and place, with mildly hypophonic speech. Cranial nerves intact to gross testing. No focal motor deficit was elicited; tone was increased in both lower limbs consistent with deconditioning. No tremor or myoclonus observed at rest. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable. 

Recent tests: Routine bloods from last month showed normal full blood count and renal profile; sodium was 136 mmol/L. Carbamazepine level not recently checked. No new neuroimaging since prior CT head 18 months ago (age-related changes only). 

Plan:
- Continue Carbamazepine 400 mg twice daily without change at present, given the single breakthrough event after a 4‑month remission and the overall tolerability reported by the care team.
- Arrange Carbamazepine trough level, liver function tests, and repeat electrolytes (including sodium) in the next two weeks via community phlebotomy.
- Request an ambulatory EEG at home if feasible through community neurophysiology to better characterise events, acknowledging the practical challenges of his bedbound status.
- Care plan: the nurse will continue the seizure log, documenting duration, features (awareness, automatisms, lateralising signs), any triggers (sleep loss, intercurrent illness), and post‑ictal recovery time.
- Safety: maintain supervised medication administration; ensure regular bowel care and hydration; continue pressure area care as per current plan. No driving or machinery operation applies given his bedbound status.
- Education: the care team were advised on recognising prolonged events (>5 minutes) or clusters, and to seek urgent medical attention if these occur or if there is concern for injury or aspiration.
- Review: telephone review in 8 weeks to assess for any further events and to discuss blood results; earlier contact if additional seizures are witnessed. 

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require further information or if his clinical status changes. 

Yours sincerely

On Carbamazepine monotherapy he was seizure-free for 4 months, until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Key statements: "On Carbamazepine monotherapy he was seizure-free for 4 months, until a focal impaired-awareness seizure occurred three Thursdays ago... There have been no further definite events since that date." He is not seizure free for ≥6 months, so we must express frequency. The current ascertainable rate over the recent period is 1 seizure in the last 4 months (a 4‑month remission followed by a single event, with none since). Choose the highest frequency mentioned; the only explicit quantifiable interval is 1 in 4 months.
